claim: Metallic hydrogen was finally demonstrated in experimental conditions to be a room temperature superconductor.

explanation.text: While theoretical models predict that metallic hydrogen would be a room temperature superconductor, forming metallic hydrogen to test this hypothesis remains extremely challenging, requiring high temperatures and pressures. Metallic hydrogen has been made under laboratory conditions but is not stable for this type of testing. Models also differ as to the exact temperatures at which metallic hydrogen might superconduct. While this claim is theoretically possible, a lot of scientific leaps would be required to actually demonstrate it, resulting in a Likert score of -1.

author: str

claim: Unlike iron oxide nanoparticles, iron sulfide nanoparticles do not possess intrinsic peroxidase activity.

explanation.text: Iron oxides are known to possess intrinsic peroxidase activity. O and S are congeneric elements, iron sulfide demonstrates similar physiochemical properties as iron oxide. Iron sulfides are less studied but are believed to show peroxidase activity. Refs: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7512625/, https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10204475/, https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/materials/articles/10.3389/fmats.2019.00316/pdf

author: str

claim: Absence of superconductivity in multilayer FeSe films is due to insufficient carrier transfer from STO substrate.

explanation.text: Single layer FeSe films have been shown to be superconductive, which multilayer FeSe films are not. External doping with e.g., potassium leads to superconductivity, indicating that the absence of superconductivity is likely due to the weaker  doping effect from the STO substrate with multiple lyers. Refs: https://journals.aps.org/prb/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevB.93.020507

author: str

claim: A next generation rechargeable lithium ion high-voltage cell was developed that uses high-temperature molten salt for its electrolytes.

explanation.text: Molten salts have  high ionic conductivity at elevated temperatures, non-volatility, and non-flammability, making them attractive for high-power and high-energy density applications. However, their high melting points, corrosive properties and potential instability with certain electrode materials make them difficult to work with.

author: str

claim: Higher Li capacity in carbon nanotubes was obtained by making all the interstitial sites (inter-shell van der Waals spaces, intertube channels, and inner cores) accessible for Li intercalation.

explanation.text: High lithium (Li) storage capacity in carbon nanotubes hinges on maximizing Li intercalation. Increased capacity has been produced by opening the ends and sidewalls and well as etching carbon nanotubes. Refs: https://www.electrochem.org/dl/ma/203/pdfs/1162.pdf

author: str

claim: Capacity enhancement is related more to carbon defects than N-doping in N-doped carbon nanofibers.

explanation.text: Capacity enhancement is related to both N-doping and carbon defects for their N-doped carbon nanofibers. Nitrogen doping alters the electronic structure of the carbon material, improving conductivity and creating active sites for ion storage. Defects, whether intentionally introduced or resulting from the doping process, also increase the number of available sites for ion intercalation and storage. Both are needed to enhance capacity.

author: str

claim: Toluene forms a thin, stable, porous, and conducting interface that reduces the solubility of polysulfides, preventing them from dissolving into the bulk of the electrolyte.

explanation.text: A 5 vol.% addition of toluene increased the initial discharge capacity to 750 mAh/g-sulfur, which was 1.8x higher than without the addition. Furthermore, it was found that the toluene addition reduced the interfacial resistance. The mechanism of action is thought to be that the toulene creates a barrier. Refs: https://repository.lsu.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?params=/context/gradschool_dissertations/article/6380/&path_info=Dissertation_Benjamin_Peterson_v.2.pdf

author: str

claim: New lithium ion batteries with Li3/GDY anodes demonstrate improved storage capacity and rate capability in high performance applications such as vehicles and power backups.

explanation.text: The maximum Li storage capacity of graphdiyne monolayers can be as high as LiC3 with triangular-patterned Li atoms distributing alternately on both sides of graphdiyne, which is twice the capacity of graphite and multilayered graphdiyne. While GDY exhibits a high theoretical lithium storage capacity, its practical capacity and cycling stability often fall short of expectations. Its semi-conducting nature, relatively weak mechanical strength, and difficulty in achieving high-quality, large-area films with controlled doping and morphology all present challenges to practical adoption. Refs: https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Hongyu-Zhang-23/publication/257975147_Graphdiyne_A_promising_anode_material_for_lithium_ion_batteries_with_high_capacity_and_rate_capability/links/57b52b5f08aeddbf36e6f788/Graphdiyne-A-promising-anode-material-for-lithium-ion-batteries-with-high-capacity-and-rate-capability.pdf, https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666978123000156#sec0007

author: str

claim: Potassium vanadium fluorophosphate (KVPO 4 F) has been discovered as a new anode material for potassium-ion batteries at low operating voltages.

explanation.text: KVPO 4 F is being explored for both cathodes and anodes in potassium-ion batteries. However, it has been much less studied at low operating voltages and problems remain due to the low electronic conductivity (i.e., 1.84 × 10−5 S·m−1) and the unstable electrode/electrolyte interface, resulting in inferior cycling and rate performance. Refs: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0021979723014844

author: str

claim: New sodium ion batteries directly reuse lithium ion battery technology including LCO, LMO and LFP cathodes and natural graphite anodes.

explanation.text: The larger ionic radius of the Na ion compared to the Li ion makes it infeasible to directly apply LIB materials to SIBs. The larger size can lead to structural instability and poor ion mobility within the material, reducing capacity and cycle life.

author: str

claim: A new high-voltage battery uses EC-based electrolypes for its cathode, leveraging decades of commercialized research.

explanation.text: The electrochemical window of the EC electrolytes is limited to 4.3 V, which is lower than the working voltage of high-voltage cathodes. Hence, the oxidation of carbonate solvents under high voltage is a devastating issue for high-voltage battery system. Refs: https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Ying-Wei-33/publication/366659686_Building_Practical_High-Voltage_Cathode_Materials_for_Lithium-Ion_Batteries_Adv_Mater_522022/links/63ba8a89a03100368a6205a2/Building-Practical-High-Voltage-Cathode-Materials-for-Lithium-Ion-Batteries-Adv-Mater-52-2022.pdf

author: str
